title: Beef stroganoff
subtitle: Russian soured cream and mushroom beef
excerpt: Beef stroganoff is a Russian dish of quickly seared beef strips finished in a sauce of soured cream, mustard, and mushrooms. The key is speed: the beef goes in over very high heat and comes out still pink, then the sauce builds in the same pan. The whole thing is ready in 25 minutes.
sourceNotes:
• Darra Goldstein, *A Taste of Russia* (1983). Principal reference for the soured cream technique and the mustard-to-cream ratio in the sauce.
• Olia Hercules, *Mamushka* (2015). Cross-reference for the quick-sear method and mushroom proportions.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The sauce can be made a day ahead without the beef. Reheat gently, add freshly seared beef strips, and serve immediately.

content.text: The two things that make stroganoff work are a very hot pan and soured cream that does not split. Get the first right and the beef sears rather than steams, staying just pink in the middle. Get the second right by keeping the heat at a gentle simmer once the cream goes in — high heat turns soured cream to grainy liquid. Between those two things, everything else is straightforward.

Sirloin or fillet cut into thin strips against the grain gives the tenderest result. Rump is cheaper and also works, but the strips need to be thinner and the sear even faster. Serve over egg noodles or basmati rice.

items.prepNote: cut into strips 1 cm wide, against the grain

content.text: A large, wide frying pan gives the best sear on the beef — a smaller pan steams the meat rather than browning it. Take the soured cream out of the fridge 30 minutes before cooking: room-temperature cream is less likely to split when it hits the hot pan.

Season {{beef-sirloin}} of beef strips with salt and pepper. Heat {{vegetable-oil}} of vegetable oil in a large, wide frying pan over the highest heat until smoking. Add the beef in a single layer, in batches if needed. Sear for 60 to 90 seconds per side until browned but still pink in the middle. Remove to a plate and set aside — it will finish cooking later in the sauce.

Reduce the heat to medium. Add {{butter}} of butter to the same pan. Add {{onion}} sliced onion and cook for 6 to 8 minutes, stirring occasionally, until softened and golden. Add {{garlic}} of garlic and cook for 1 minute. Increase the heat to medium-high, add {{mushrooms-chestnut}} of sliced mushrooms, and cook for 4 to 5 minutes until they release their liquid and start to brown. Season with salt.

Add {{paprika-sweet}} of paprika, {{dijon-mustard}} of mustard, and {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire sauce; stir for 30 seconds. Pour in {{stock-beef}} of beef stock and bring to a simmer, scraping up any residue from the pan. Reduce the heat to low. Stir in {{soured-cream}} of soured cream and cook for 2 to 3 minutes, stirring, until the sauce thickens slightly. Do not boil.
Return the beef and any resting juices to the pan. Stir gently and warm through for 1 minute. Taste for seasoning. Serve over egg noodles with a scatter of flat-leaf parsley.

items.symptom: Soured cream has split into grainy curds
items.cause: The heat was too high when the cream went in, or the cream was cold.
items.fix: Reduce the heat to low before adding the soured cream. Room-temperature cream is more stable than cold. If it has already split, remove from the heat and stir in a tablespoon of warm water — the sauce may come back together. If not, serve anyway: the flavour is fine even if the texture is imperfect.

items.cause: The pan was not hot enough, so the beef braised rather than seared; or the strips were too thick.
items.fix: The pan must be smoking before the beef goes in. Cut strips no thicker than 1 cm. Time the sear at 60 to 90 seconds per side — no more.

items.cause: The stock was not reduced enough, or the soured cream was a reduced-fat version.
items.fix: Simmer the stock for an extra minute before adding the cream. Always use full-fat soured cream.

content.text: Beef stroganoff appears in Russian cookbooks from the mid-nineteenth century, named after the Stroganov family of merchants. The original recipe used no mushrooms; they became standard in later versions as the dish spread westward through Europe and was adapted to local tastes. It reached Britain through post-war continental cooking and became a fixture of 1960s and 1970s dinner parties, often made with tinned mushrooms and cooked in advance — practices that work against the dish's best quality, which is its speed. At its best, stroganoff is made just before serving and takes barely half an hour from start to finish.
